title: Feed thumbnail source-domain labels render as invisible white text over the light image placeholder
description: Each feed post card overlays the story's source domain (e.g. "tombedor.dev", "innovativegenomics.org") as white text in the bottom-right corner of the thumbnail. The label has color:#fff, text-shadow:none, and a transparent background, and the thumbnail button's placeholder background is light gray (bg-[#ececf1], rgb(236,236,241)). While the image is loading — and permanently for any post whose thumbnail fails to load (the Unsplash image photo-1461784180009 returns HTTP 404) — the white label sits on the light-gray placeholder at a contrast ratio of ~1.18:1 and is effectively invisible. Because the label has no scrim/gradient/text-shadow, its legibility also remains poor over light-colored loaded images.
expected_behavior: The source-domain label should remain legible regardless of the thumbnail's state — e.g. a dark gradient scrim or text-shadow behind the label, or a non-white label color over light placeholders — so it never drops below the WCAG AA 4.5:1 contrast minimum.
actual_behavior: During thumbnail loading (and permanently for the 404 thumbnail), the source-domain label is white text on the light-gray placeholder (~1.18:1) and cannot be read. The screenshot at 4967ms shows "tombedor.dev" and "innovativegenomics.org" as ghost text barely distinguishable from the gray box.

chain.text: The thumbnail placeholder color #ececf1 stays behind the label until the image loads, and for the post whose thumbnail requests photo-1461784180009 the image returns HTTP 404 (twice), so the placeholder — and therefore the invisible-label state — is permanent for that card.

evidence.result:

chronology.text: The Digg feed loads. Post thumbnails are still being fetched, so each thumbnail shows its light-gray placeholder box (bg-[#ececf1]).

chronology.text: While the thumbnails are on their light-gray placeholders, the white source-domain labels "tombedor.dev" and "innovativegenomics.org" are overlaid on them and are effectively invisible — only faint ghost text is visible against the gray boxes.

evidence.result:

chronology.text: Once images load, the same labels appear over the photos, but because there is no scrim the white label over the light-toned robot image ("tombedor.dev") remains low contrast and hard to read. For posts whose image 404s, the placeholder never resolves and the label stays invisible.

evidence.result:
Screenshot at timestamp 13785: https://static.replay.io/recordings/d1875d77-8752-429b-b3ce-72f624da8bb7/analysis/screenshot-13785.jpg
Next execution point: Point:5 @ 13814.50ms

root_cause.text: The domain-label span is styled color:rgb(255,255,255) with text-shadow:none and background:rgba(0,0,0,0) (transparent), positioned absolutely over a thumbnail button whose class includes bg-[#ececf1] (rgb(236,236,241)). Evaluating the live DOM confirms these computed styles. With no text-shadow or background scrim, white-on-#ececf1 yields ~1.18:1 contrast, so the label is invisible whenever the placeholder color (rather than a dark image) is behind it.

evidence.result: "[{\"color\":\"rgb(255, 255, 255)\",\"textShadow\":\"none\",\"bg\":\"rgba(0, 0, 0, 0)\",\"parentBg\":\"rgb(236, 236, 241)\",\"parentClass\":\"relative mt-2 hidden h-[174px] w-[344px] shrink-0 overflow-hidden rounded-[18px] bg-[#ececf1] shadow-[0_20px_40px_rgba(20,20,24,0.12)] lg:block\"}]"

analysis.buggy_behavior: The source-domain label overlaid on each feed thumbnail is white text with no scrim or shadow; over the light-gray image placeholder it renders at ~1.18:1 contrast and is invisible to the user.

judge_assessment.assessment:
Evidence: Screenshot 1 (Image 1) shows the Digg feed in a loading state with 'Loading stories...' text and no post cards visible yet — consistent with the chronology claim that thumbnails are still being fetched. Screenshot 2 (Image 2) shows two post cards with light-gray placeholder boxes where thumbnails should be. The source-domain labels ('tombedor.dev' and 'innovativegenomics.org') are barely visible as faint white text in the bottom-right corners of these gray placeholders, confirming the claimed invisible-label glitch. Screenshot 3 (Image 3) shows the same posts with images loaded — the 'tombedor.dev' label over the light-toned robot image remains hard to read due to poor contrast, while 'innovativegenomics.org' over the darker CRISPR image is more legible but still lacks a proper scrim. The root cause evidence from the Evaluate tool confirms the specific computed styles: color rgb(255,255,255), textShadow 'none', background rgba(0,0,0,0), and parentBg rgb(236,236,241) with the class bg-[#ececf1] — all matching the claims in the root cause text. The NetworkRequest evidence confirms 2 requests to 'photo-1461784180009' both returning 4xx status, supporting the claim about permanent placeholder state for failed thumbnails. The contrast ratio claim of ~1.18:1 is a reasonable calculation from white (#ffffff) on #ececf1. All factual claims are traceable to evidence results. The report precisely identifies the affected UI element (source-domain label spans), their location (bottom-right of thumbnail), and provides both visual and DOM-level evidence.

Requirements: The bug report documents a genuine invisible-text issue that matches the project's guidelines precisely. In Image 2 (timestamp 4967), the thumbnails show light-gray placeholders with the source-domain labels ('tombedor.dev', 'innovativegenomics.org') rendered as white text that is effectively invisible against the gray background — you can barely make out the text in the bottom-right corners. In Image 3 (timestamp 13785), after images load, the labels become somewhat more visible but 'tombedor.dev' over the light-toned robot image still has poor contrast. The root cause is well-supported by DOM evaluation evidence showing color:rgb(255,255,255) on a transparent background over a parent with bg-[#ececf1] (rgb(236,236,241)), yielding ~1.18:1 contrast ratio. The network evidence confirms the Unsplash image returns HTTP 404 (2 failed requests), making the invisible state permanent for that card. This is exactly the type of invisible-text finding the guidelines prioritize as critical.
